summary: Joe Reis shares Zach Wilson's observations from Stockholm about the state of data engineering in 2026, arguing that AI innovation is no longer confined to San Francisco, that dashboard-focused roles are declining, and that data engineers should focus on the 'Three Vs' (volume, velocity, variety) to remain relevant in an AI-augmented world.
key_points: Dashboards and traditional BI visualization work is declining in value and not future-proof
The Three Vs (Volume, Velocity, Variety) are a data engineer's moat against AI displacement
AI best practices haven't been established yet, making it harder to teach than data engineering
Under-specified prompts are the biggest skill gap in AI usage
AI innovation is genuinely happening outside San Francisco, with cities like Stockholm showing real energy and talent
money_quotes: Building a Tableau viz so an exec can stare at it for ten seconds is not a future-proof career. The work has moved on.
If you're a data engineer working only with low-volume, batch, structured data, you are exposed. Pick a V and go deep. Preferably, stack two of them.
AI is hard to teach because the best practices haven't been established.
The gap between what actually works and what vendors are selling you is wide, and vendors are very happy to live in it.
Give it 10% more context, and you get 80% better output. This is not subtle. Under-specified prompts are the single biggest skill gap he sees.
key_insight: Data engineers should future-proof their careers by moving toward high-volume, real-time, and multimodal data work — the dimensions where AI can't yet replace human expertise.
